---
title: "Introduction to Shiny Modules"
---

## Apps grow into tangled graphs

![](/img/modules-before.png){height="500px" fig-align="center"}

::: {.footnote}
Source: [Mastering Shiny, ch. 19](https://mastering-shiny.org/scaling-modules.html)
:::

## Group the pieces that belong together

![](/img/modules-after.png){height="500px" fig-align="center"}

## What changed

- Related boxes get grouped into a named unit (`session`, `data`, `results`, ...)
- The **same shape** (yellow) shows up twice: written once, used twice
- The pitch for modules: **isolate** pieces so you can reason about them
  alone, **reuse** pieces so you don't rewrite them

## A module is just a function

- Abstraction is why we write functions at all: hide the details, expose a
  small interface, reuse the logic without re-deriving it
- A plain function already gives you that

So why isn't it enough here?

## The same problem, at our scale

- Your tips dashboard's sidebar: a slider, a checkbox group, and a
  `@reactive.calc` that filters the data
- Imagine a second dataset with its own filters: copy-paste the sidebar
  code, copy-paste the filter logic
- Now there are **two places** to fix every bug and **two places** to add
  every new feature

## Functions don't fully solve this

- Remember: every input/output **ID must be unique across the whole app**
- Python's function scoping gives two calls independent local variables for
  free
- But `"filter_total_bill"` isn't a variable: it's a string shipped to the
  browser and tracked in Shiny's session-wide input list, and Python's
  scope has no jurisdiction over it
- Call the function twice and you get two `input_slider()`s both named
  `"filter_total_bill"`: Shiny can't tell them apart

## Shiny modules

A module is a function that:

- Encapsulates UI *and* server logic together
- Gets its own **namespace**: an ID prefix Shiny manages for you, so
  duplicate inner IDs never collide
- Otherwise behaves like a normal Python function: takes arguments,
  returns values

<https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/modules.html>

## Shiny Express: `@module`

- One function is both UI and server
- Modules have been supported in Express since Shiny 0.10.0

```{.python}
from shiny.express import module, render, ui

@module
def scatter_panel(input, output, session, data, x, y):
    ...
```

Call it like a normal function, plus an id:
`scatter_panel("bill_depth_mm_bill_length_mm", data=dat, x="bill_depth_mm", y="bill_length_mm")`.

::: {.callout-note}
Shiny Core has the same idea, split into `@module.ui` / `@module.server`.
We'll stick to Express here.
:::

## Our scatter module

One scatterplot panel with its own "Points" vs "Density" toggle, built once
and used once per pair of columns:

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| components: [editor, viewer]
#| layout: vertical
#| viewerHeight: 550

{{< include /../code/demos/app-06-modules_scatter_combos.py >}}
```

- Each panel's `view` id is namespaced by its own `f"{x}_{y}"` instance id
- Toggling one panel never touches any other
- Nothing here was hand-rolled to make that true

## Recap

- A module is a function with its own namespace: call it in a loop, on a
  second dataset, anywhere, and its inner IDs never collide
- `x`, `y`, and `data` are just arguments, captured normally on each call
- Same idea works from Shiny Core or Shiny Express
